author: @KeyboardBuster
text:
This is similar to the (or the same in every way that matters) to the induction lamps of the 80s from Philips. They were up in the high khz to low MHz range, I think they wrapped their wire around a ferrite rod and slid it into a cavity in a florecent bulb like what's shown here.
The only thing that "wears out" is the driver they used whether external or built in.

author: @douglascalhoun6471
text: Always fascinating to see unorthodox approaches to tried and true circuits. It reminds me of some prototypes of 5mm led lights I made 16 or 17 years ago that made quite a bit of light while using only battery power. Of course I was only driving the leds around 6 or 7 ma and using a great many leds to get the volume of light. Interesting to see where this leads to.

author: @lesmaybury793
text:
"Sack the camera man!" 😂😂😂.
One of the tinkering things I have been doing is scavaging discarded vape batteries. They are Li-ion batteries that are rechargeable. Currently I have made little rechargeable power packs to power led Xmas lights.
Using a 3.7 vape battery boosted to 12v usingca buck module may well work. The ones I collected range from 360 to 850 mah so should givexa bit of run time. They can always be matched and wired in parallel to increase capacity.
More thinking required.

I think that might look quite cool, especially if you could drive a tiny motor to do the rotating with the slayer itself, so no wires connecting it.

author: @Clancydaenlightened
text:
4:10 two ways 

Use a feed back coil or couple the secondary 0v end to a pair mosfet gate drivers, one inverted, and use a gate drive transformer with a half bridge with power mosfets or igbt depending on your resonant frequency 


Or use a zero volt switching driver, look up mazzilli zvs

author: @scotttovey

author: @stephenroot1012
text: This is almost perfect. I have done something similar using a plasma bulb for the exciter power but this is even better. If you can beef the exciter power circuit to about 8 watts and keep the electrodes from discharging into the mercury vapor you might can avoid the dark spots that show up on fluorescent tubes as they get dimmer. Even better if you can avoid using tubes from China which supposedly leaks UV light.

replies.author: @stephenroot1012
replies.text: Since I posted my comment I had a look at a cf bulb and on second thought it may need to be boosted to 15 watts, maybe a bit more, depending on losses and the type of tube used.

author: @simonexperiences
text:
always great!
I love high-voltage machines! next step, the plasma candle?
off-topic...: as winter is here: any chance; has anyone ever tested the ionic wind and ozone (in corona discharge) in a rocket stove?
would ozonated oil make a good fuel for a stove?

replies.author: @taylorwestmore4664
replies.text: Someone on YouTube tried to recreate the electrokinetic flame jet generator patents of Thomas Townsend Brown. They had modest success but they only ran the ion generating needle electrode and blew air through the nozzle instead of using jet fuel exhaust. In principle you could design a similar high voltage feedback circuit for a rocket stove and get over 2 million volts with the appropriately constructed circuit.

replies.author: @simonexperiences
replies.text:
@taylorwestmore4664 
merci pour l'info, j'ai regardé un peu ses pattents, très intéressant (surtout son moteur électrostatique)!
mais j’imaginai quelque chose de plus simple et low cost pour booster la combustion...
je m'aperçois qu'un ventilateur pc consomme moins de watts...
mais l'ozone devrait faire un petit quelque chose sur les fumées et la combustion ;-)
merci

author: @onbedoeldekut1515
text:
This makes me think of a guitar product called Lace (the company) 'Alumitone' pickups.
Instead of being 'regular' thousands of winds around a magnet/poles, there's an element with only a few hundred, (compared to thousands), and what appear to be capacitors.
I'm now thinking this must be the base signal amplification aspect of the device.
